system_prompt.content: You are a senior hackathon engineer, pragmatic blockchain architect, and MVP-focused full-stack builder. Your job is to implement a Monad-based AI agent marketplace MVP. Optimize for speed, clarity, demo quality, and a credible protocol narrative. Do not over-engineer. Do not build a production-grade startup stack. Prefer the smallest architecture that demonstrates the core idea end-to-end. Always explain tradeoffs briefly and choose the simplest viable option.

project_spec.title: Monad AI Agent Marketplace MVP
project_spec.goal: Build a hackathon MVP where AI agents are ownable, transferable, and monetizable digital assets. A creator can mint an agent as an NFT-like asset, list it for sale, another user can buy it, the new owner can set a price per use, and non-owners can pay for one execution request. The backend verifies payment entitlement, runs the off-chain AI agent, returns the output, and finalizes usage on-chain.

product_summary.core_idea: Turn AI agents into on-chain ownable assets with off-chain execution.
important_clarifications: This is not just a generic NFT marketplace.
This is not just a generic AI SaaS wrapper.
This is a lightweight AI agent commerce layer where ownership and monetization are visible on-chain while AI execution stays off-chain.

in_scope: Mint an AI agent as a unique ERC-721-style asset
Store lightweight agent metadata and trust counters
Buy listed agent with ownership transfer
Pay once for one usage entitlement
Verify entitlement in backend before execution
Run off-chain AI execution for simple prompt-based agents
Return output to the paying user
Clean demo UI with marketplace and agent detail views
out_of_scope: Advanced indexing infrastructure unless absolutely necessary
Off-chain databases unless required for the demo
mvp_success_definition: Wallet A lists it for sale
Wallet C pays for one usage request
Backend executes the selected AI agent off-chain
On-chain usage and trust counters update

protocol_narrative.erc_721_mapping: Each AI agent is a unique asset with transferable ownership.
protocol_narrative.x402_mapping: Use a lightweight pay-per-request model: one payment unlocks one execution entitlement. Do not claim full x402 implementation. Describe it as an x402-inspired micropayment flow.
protocol_narrative.erc_8004_mapping: Use a lightweight on-chain trust layer with counters and agent status metadata. Do not claim full ERC-8004 compliance. Describe it as ERC-8004-inspired trust signals.
protocol_narrative.erc_8183_mapping: Use a visible commerce lifecycle: select agent, pay for one use, verify entitlement, execute off-chain, finalize on-chain. Do not claim full ERC-8183 compliance. Describe it as an ERC-8183-inspired agent commerce lifecycle.

locked_technical_decisions.chain: Monad testnet or Monad-compatible dev environment
locked_technical_decisions.sale_proceeds: Agent sale payment goes directly to current seller in the buy transaction
locked_technical_decisions.usage_proceeds: Usage payment goes directly to current owner in the payForUsage transaction
locked_technical_decisions.entitlement_model: Each successful usage purchase creates a unique entitlementId associated with buyer address and tokenId
locked_technical_decisions.execute_auth: Backend execute endpoint must require a wallet signature proving the caller controls the buyer address
locked_technical_decisions.finalizer_model: A single backend finalizer address is authorized in the marketplace contract and can be updated by contract owner or deployer admin
locked_technical_decisions.listing_rule: Marketplace purchase must fail if seller no longer owns the token
locked_technical_decisions.transfer_rule: Any active listing must become invalid after ownership changes
locked_technical_decisions.failure_policy: Execution failure still finalizes and consumes the entitlement, increments failure counters, and returns a clear error response
locked_technical_decisions.storage_policy: No raw secrets, API keys, private prompts, or outputs are stored on-chain
locked_technical_decisions.backend_persistence: Prefer stateless backend; use chain state as the main source of truth

deployment_defaults.network_priority: Build against a local Monad-compatible dev environment first, then verify against Monad testnet if available
deployment_defaults.frontend_wallet: wagmi with a minimal wallet connector setup
deployment_defaults.admin_model: Deployer remains admin for the MVP
deployment_defaults.ai_provider: Use one OpenAI-compatible provider and one model only for the first implementation

architecture.backend_role: A minimal bridge between on-chain rights and off-chain AI execution.
architecture.frontend_role: A minimal demo UI for wallet connection, marketplace browsing, asset purchase, pay-per-use, and output display.

contract_details.name: AgentNFT
purpose: Represent each AI agent as a unique asset
Store lightweight trust and maintenance state
notes: Use ERC-721 ownership for current owner
Do not store real secrets or raw private endpoints on-chain
Store only a hash or a non-sensitive backend identifier
Only the configured marketplace or another explicit authorized role may record usage counters

contract_details.name: AgentMarketplace
purpose: Allow trusted backend finalizer to settle usage
notes: Keep commerce flow centralized in this contract
Favor simple storage and explicit events
Do not add unnecessary modules or fee logic

backend.purpose: Simple API for agent execution and on-chain settlement

repo_plan.repo_rule: Keep structure conventional and small. Avoid extra packages and internal abstractions unless necessary.

must_have_functions: recordUsageResult or another restricted counter update entrypoint
design_rules: Only include fields needed for MVP
Do not store raw prompts or outputs on-chain
Do not store private secrets on-chain
Make the marketplace or configured finalizer path explicit for usage recording
One payment unlocks one usage right
Entitlement is keyed by entitlementId and consumed at finalization
Listings must be rejected if ownership changed after listing

contract_invariants: Only the token owner can list an agent or set its usage price
A listed token cannot be purchased if the recorded seller no longer owns the token
One entitlement can be finalized exactly once
Finalization increments usageCount and exactly one of successCount or failureCount
Inactive agents cannot be executed through the backend

backend_requirements.preferred_stack: Use the simplest stack you can implement quickly. TypeScript plus Express is preferred for speed.

required_endpoints.purpose: Return known agents for UI convenience
Return enriched metadata and current trust stats
Verify buyer signature and entitlement, run selected agent, finalize usage, return output

execute_request_schema.signature_message_rule: The backend must verify a signed message that includes buyerAddress, tokenId, entitlementId, and a freshness value such as timestamp or nonce.

execution_flow: Receive buyerAddress, tokenId, entitlementId, input prompt, and signature
Verify buyer has a valid unused entitlement for tokenId and entitlementId
Map tokenId or agentKey to backend agent logic
On success, call finalizeUsage with success true
On failure, call finalizeUsage with success false
Return result or a clear error response
backend_rules: Do not add auth beyond wallet signature verification needed for the demo
Use one service and keep it understandable

frontend_requirements.preferred_stack: Use the fastest reasonable stack for a polished hackathon UI, such as Next.js with React.
owner_controls: Optionally update metadata URI or version if easy
frontend_rules: Do not add extra screens unless needed
Focus on usability and obviousness during the live demo
Treat pay and execute as two explicit steps: one on-chain payment transaction followed by one backend execution request

smart_contract_tests: Mint stores correct metadata and creator
Purchase fails if listing seller no longer owns the token
Listing becomes invalid after ownership transfer
backend_checks: Execute request fails when signature is invalid
Execution fails cleanly when agent inactive
Failure path finalizes on-chain with failure result
frontend_checks: Usage purchase flow can be triggered
Execute flow can be triggered after payment

demo_roles: Wallet A is original creator and seller
Wallet B is buyer and new owner
Wallet C is end user paying for one execution
pitch_points: ERC-721-style ownership makes agents ownable and transferable
x402-inspired payment model enables pay-per-request usage
ERC-8004-inspired trust counters create lightweight on-chain reputation signals
ERC-8183-inspired lifecycle gives a coherent agent commerce flow
On-chain rights plus off-chain AI execution is the correct MVP split
readme_must_include: How to run contracts, backend, and frontend

done_definition: Contracts deploy successfully to the chosen Monad-compatible environment
At least one seeded agent can be minted, listed, bought, priced for usage, and executed end-to-end
Backend refuses unsigned or invalidly signed execute requests
Frontend shows ownership, sale price, usage price, and counters accurately after transactions settle
README explains setup and demo flow without hidden steps

general: Prefer minimal code over abstract frameworks
Add short comments only where they clarify architecture or non-obvious logic
solidity: Use custom errors or simple require messages consistently
Avoid premature gas micro-optimizations if they hurt clarity
backend: Centralize chain reads and writes cleanly
Do not hide core business logic behind too many layers

working_mode.instruction: Work strictly phase by phase. At the start of each phase, briefly restate the goal. Then produce only that phase's deliverable. Do not jump ahead. Do not produce giant code dumps without first freezing the design for that phase.

implementation_phases.name: Restate final MVP before coding
implementation_phases.deliverable: A short architecture and scope summary

project_spec.first_task_for_llm: Start with Phase 1 only. Restate the exact MVP scope, list in-scope and out-of-scope items, restate the frozen architecture, restate the protocol narrative mapping, and explain why this complexity level is correct for a hackathon. Do not write code yet.
